learning_outcomes.description: Recognise and explain the principal philosophical / ethical theories associated with the human-animal relationship.
Identify and understand the principal legal and regulatory frameworks by which human-animal relationships are managed.
Critically analyse the objectives of those legal and regulatory frameworks in light of both philosophical theories and international jurisprudence.
Understand how Commonwealth, State and Local Council laws and regulations are intended to work together in regulating animals.
Identify the rights and obligations imposed upon individuals and other legal entities under these Commonwealth, State and Local Council regulatory regimes as they relate to the treatment of animals.
Communicate effectively, appropriately and persuasively on issues concerning animal law and regulation.
Conduct legal research, undertake reasoned debate and engage in persuasive argument concerning animal law and regulation.

assessments.due_date:
6/08/2026 - 22/10/2026

The responses will be completed during the relevant Thursday class.
assessments.category: Essay/ Critique, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessments.task_description: Each Thursday, a small set of readings (articles, cases or policy documents) will be posted to the Animal Law Blackboard site. During the first 30 minutes of the class on the following Thursday (commencing in Week 2), students will be asked to draft an open-book, 300-400 hand-written response to a question concerning those readings. The question will be provided at the start of that Thursday class. Responses will be collected and assigned a mark of up to 5%. Across the 12 weeks of the semester, students will be required to complete ten of these responses. At the end of the semester, the 7 highest results for each student will be counted for a maximum mark of 35%.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will hand-write their 300-400 word critical response to the provided question during the Thursday class. Responses will then be collected by the Instructor upon completion.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
3/09/2026 - 29/10/2026

More information about scheduling and other practical matters will be discussed closer to Week 6.
assessments.task_description: Commencing in Week 6, students will be invited to prepare and deliver a 10-15 min oral presentation on an aspect of Animal Law that particularly interests them. Topics can be drawn from the course itself, but other legal, philosophical and regulatory topics concerning Animal Law may be selected in consultation with the instructor. The idea is to collect your thoughts about an issue you are really interested in and then share your insights with the rest of us in a relaxed and open discussion forum. Students in past editions of Animal Law often report that this was their favourite aspect of the course!
assessments.submission_guidelines: Presentation during Thursday class on a date chosen by students.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date: Students will be required to submit their 500 word research methodology paper within 7 days of their oral presentation.
assessments.task_description: Students will be asked to submit a 500 word paper explaining why they chose to present on their particular topic or issue and explain how they went about researching and formulating the content of their presentation. A bibliography of sources is to be included.
assessments.submission_guidelines: A Turnitin link will be created on the subject Blackboard site for students to upload their research methodology paper.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
27/10/2026 2:00 pm

Further instructions / explanations will be provided during the semester
assessments.task_description: Students will be invited to submit a 1800 word response to a problem / factual scenario concerning animal law. The question will take the form of a certain problem / factual scenario concerning an issue within the scope of the Animal Law topics covered in the course. It will be released at the start of Week 7 and will be due on the Tuesday of Week 13. The exercise is designed to enable students to demonstrate their understanding of how relevant legal, regulatory, policy and ethical frameworks intersect in addressing and/or attempting to resolve the scenario.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will upload their paper to a Turnitin link on the subject Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 12(27 Jul - 25 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

Classes for Animal Law during Semester Two, 2026 are scheduled in "Seminar" format each Thursday from 5:00pm - 8:00pm. Each Thursday seminar will include between 3-4 activities. These activities will include (i) the presentation of material (by the instructor) relevant to the topic for the week; (ii) commencing in Week 2, writing the critical reflections about the set reading material, (iii) discussion and debate (led and moderated by the instructor) about the material relevant to the topic for the week, including relevant news items concerning animal law, (iv) commencing in Week 6, the oral presentations and associated questions and discussions.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
